He separately cautioned that [open-weights models are more fragile](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-4ab4665fc098) out-of-distribution than benchmarks suggest.

- **Nathan Lambert** anchored the open-science thread: crediting [open work shaping the field](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-aff8329e0cbe) (e.g., Tulu 3's RLVR), framing the [open-vs-closed debate](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-69b17f54b326) around the value of marginally better intelligence, and noting **Claude** seems ["lazy" in chat search](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-d524e377e223) versus **GPT-5.5**.
- **Inference infrastructure** engaged practitioners: **vLLM** [shipped v0.22.0](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-cec703ee635c) with **DeepSeek V4** hardening and a Rust frontend, while **NVIDIA** [unveiled **DynoSim**](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-c0e6469bbc91) to simulate Dynamo serving deployments.
- **OpenAI's Greg Brockman** [spotlighted AI accelerating research](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-6119d6af99f5), and **Hugging Face's Clément Delangue** [praised the **AI Security Institute**](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-f447d7d7f548) for openly releasing evals, datasets, and models.
- Economic skepticism persisted, with **Timnit Gebru** [mocking a report](/?date=2026-05-31&category=social#item-00b1fedc73e8) of a company accidentally spending $500M on **Claude** in a single month.

items.title:
There's a better way to serve your inference stack, you just haven't found it yet.

DynoSim is a wor...
items.content:
There's a better way to serve your inference stack, you just haven't found it yet.

DynoSim is a workload-driven simulation of the Dynamo serving stack that turns exhaustive deployment search into a simulate-then-verify loop.

Instead of testing every deployment choice, teams can model the whole stack on one virtual timeline, screen thousands of configurations in high fidelity simulation, then validate only the best candidates on real hardware.  

And because it's a full Rust implementation, it runs extremely fast. In our testing, 1,500x faster than real time.

items.title: Given that Claude seems so lazy in chat (especially with technical search topics), it seems pretty t...
items.content:
Given that Claude seems so lazy in chat (especially with technical search topics), it seems pretty telling about how a harness can make a model far more independent and thorough.

GPT 5.5, and many of OpenAI's recent models, seem incredibly thorough -- like they won't give up -- and the codex harness is a much lighter change on the model.

Of course I have a lot of uncertainty here, but it's surprising to me how weak Claude's search is when I try the Claude app again. I only use ChatGPT for research, but Claude Code can do wonderful things like getting exactly the right figures from papers I know and insert them into a slide deck.

Interesting times ahead!

items.title: The secret to LiteParse lies in the grid projection algorithm. We project a complex page layout with...
items.content:
The secret to LiteParse lies in the grid projection algorithm. We project a complex page layout with text and tables into well-structured text, that humans can read and agents can understanding.

This contains of a few core steps (no LLMs!): 
1. Grouping text fragments to lines
2. Identify left,center,right anchors
3. Snap each text item to an anchor 
4. Handle flowing paragraphs separately 
5. Render each text item in a carefully tuned order so that each piece of text aligns to a grid column 
6. Post-processing

For more details check out this great blog post we wrote a month ago! 

https://t.co/0SPpV2B8kr

items.summary: Jerry Liu explains the grid projection algorithm behind LiteParse, a document parsing approach that converts complex page layouts with text and tables into structured text without using LLMs, via line grouping, anchor detection, snapping and ordered rendering.

items.title:
I'm yet to see an agent running inside a browser that doesn't feel like a hack.

I tried a headless ...
items.content:
I'm yet to see an agent running inside a browser that doesn't feel like a hack.

I tried a headless browser, but I can't use my logins with it. I tried a Chrome extension, but it keeps killing my sessions.

The team behind ego's whole argument is that browsers were never built for agents, so everything we have today is a patch on top of that.

They rebuilt the entire thing, and it looks promising:

• You can run multiple agents in the same browser
• Each agent owns its own space
• Agents work in parallel
• You can watch a space, take over it, or kill it

This is all Chromium underneath, so you can keep using your extensions, bookmarks, etc.

The best part is that you aren't locked to any assistant: you can use this with Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or whatever you use.

Here is a link to check it out: https://t.co/i4t5sFxVmN

items.title:
PrismML just shrunk a 4B image model down to fit on your iPhone.

Most image generation today runs i...
items.content:
PrismML just shrunk a 4B image model down to fit on your iPhone.

Most image generation today runs in the cloud. Every prompt becomes a remote request with latency and cost.
 
PrismML released Bonsai Image 4B to change that. 

It runs diffusion inference directly on phones and laptops.
 
The team shipped two open-weight variants of a 4B-class image model. 

Each one shrinks the diffusion transformer using extreme weight quantization.
 
> 1-bit version weighs 0.93 GB
> Ternary version weighs 1.21 GB
> Both run on iPhone 17 Pro Max
> Up to 5.6x faster on M4 Pro
> Open weights for local deployment
 
The binary build hits 8.3x compression versus full precision. 

The ternary build trades size for fidelity, keeping 95% of original quality.
 
A 512x512 image takes 9.4 seconds on a phone. 

Mean memory usage stays under 2 GB during generation.
 
What happens when every device becomes a creative studio?

items.content:
You can now run a Hermes agent team from one control room. 

Running multiple AI agents usually turns into chaos. 

Credentials scattered everywhere, no clear ownership, nobody knows what runs where.
 
Hermes Agent Control Room is a public template that fixes this. 

It treats your agent fleet like an operating system instead of disconnected bots.
 
The setup grows in four stages:
 
1. One agent on a VPS with docs
2. Direct specialists for SEO, dev, ops
3. An orchestrator routing across them
4. Cron jobs running the whole team
 
You hand over a Hetzner API key. 

The bundled skills spin up the server, install tooling, clone the repo, register your first agent. Ten minutes total.
 
The control plane handles config, runbooks, secrets. 

Specialists do the work.
 
The orchestrator becomes one front door when you want it.
 
Through the gateway you can route any agent to 300+ models. 

What would you automate first?

items.content:
What if the biggest change in software isn’t a new language, but the fact that you may not need to think in code first anymore?

I think we’re moving from:
syntax → intent

items.content:
@TeslaBoomerMama Dear Ms. Merz, 

Let me clear up a few of your misapprehensions.

First, I posted publicly a few days ago about why I temporarily added my titles. It was in solidarity with a 19 year old (and ally of his) who had been bullied by the owner of this platform.  I stand by that decision.

Second, if you can justify the S-1, I am all ears. We both know that the Danish fund is *just one place among many *to find the S-1 to be outlandish. See Martin Peers in The Information, @edels0n’s long dissection etc. By focusing on the alleged bias of *one* investor you are distracting away reality. I don’t think well of that kind of tactic.

Third, your view of BYD vs Tesla I think portrays a bias of your own. Many many people take the view that BYD is making better cars for less. In any case Tesla was once utterly dominant in EVs, and that’s just not the case anymore.

Finally, I made no claim as to whether Waymo is ever going to be profitable. That’s just putting words in my mouth; if you were to take the time to read my writings you would find that I don’t see full level 5 self-driving is near, for any of the players.

If you are going to come at me, please do you research first. 

Clearly you did not.

There is a lot to hate about these. 

“AI pendants with cameras and microphones.”

While that is tru...
items.content:
There is a lot to hate about these. 

“AI pendants with cameras and microphones.”

While that is true, wearable cameras do have a good part to them. Although I can not yet recommend them to others. At least not strongly. Am studying why not, and writing them a report.

Yesterday went for a walk with @Super_PK_ in San Francisco and ended up on this bench at a park across the street from @hf0 startup residency. Had a beautiful view of SF behind me.

Wearing a @Looki_ai pendant around my neck. Pawel and I talked about it and his business.

Microsoft researchers in San Francisco pioneered the form back in 2005.

Today the AI sent me this video of the memory and told me to touch grass like this more often.

Pawel is building a business for dog owners in Chicago, Illinois.

Earlier in the day I hung out with a few entrepreneurs visiting SF from India.

It is why I love San Francisco. 

Gold rushes bring people from all over the world.

Back to the pendant. Once you get over the freaky factor it makes fun videos of the best of your day.

And writes you a journal with advice.

What is it missing?

It is like the Ferrari: it didn’t go into the freaky future enough.

Ferrari has self driving technology that goes 250 m.p.h. I saw it in Abu Dhabi. If Ferrari had put that into the new Luce we would have had a much different conversation today. 

The Looki could go ultra freaky.

But would require everyone to get one and go completely post privacy. Like I have. Grok knows everything about me.

Not gonna happen until Apple gets enough of us to wear them.

Could sense we are both having a good time in the park and automatically connect us on X. 

But would require us to cross all the freaky lines. I don’t even know if Apple can convince that many.

:-)

→ re...
items.content:
What makes this interesting to me is not just faster prototyping.

It’s the stack compression:

→ real data connections
→ interactive tools
→ live collaboration
→ instant deployment, without wrestling infrastructure

That means founders can validate faster.
Operators can turn ideas into working tools.
Developers can spend more energy on outcomes, not boilerplate.

This is why “vibe coding” is more than a trend word, it’s a workflow shift.

items.title:
The old model was simple:
you had an idea,
then translated it into syntax,
then fought the tooling u...
items.content:
The old model was simple:
you had an idea,
then translated it into syntax,
then fought the tooling until it worked.

The new model looks very different:

→ describe the app in plain language
→ generate the interface, logic, and structure
→ test it
→ refine it through feedback

That loop matters.

When iteration becomes conversational, speed stops being reserved for highly specialized teams.

items.title:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes.   After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as:  These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

References 

Khan, A. A., Hasan, M. T., Kemell, K. K., Rasku, J., & Abrahamsson, P. (2025). Developing retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based LLM systems from PDFs: An experience report [Preprint]. arXiv. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YCGfBCNY4v

Santos, G. (2025, March 3). LLM + RAG: Creating an AI-powered file reader assistant. Towards Data Science. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YubIqDgyP8

items.title:
Companies in Europe get a few things for doing this:

1) extreme cost savings: they don't have to st...
items.content:
Companies in Europe get a few things for doing this:

1) extreme cost savings: they don't have to stock amenities in every room, wash towels, clean rooms every day, putting AC at minimum 23-25C lowers their energy bills, which all increases their profits by a lot

2) corporations that do group bookings are/were pushed to be ESG (environmental, social, and governance), aka eco friendly, hotels could/can attract those corporations by being eco

3) they get tax cuts on investments in their property if it's eco, environmental or sustainable

But the quality of stays drops by a lot for people, so it's more about appealing to companies and governments and saving money, than appealing to guests

Which is misaligned incentives, as the end user of a hotel is the guest, who is now sleeping bad in a sweaty room with minimal AC, no amenities, no daily room cleaning and minimal service
